history: The client has a history of physical health challenges, including a recent shoulder surgery with ongoing pain and numbness from a herniated cervical disc. They previously experienced immobility due to illness and have undergone physical therapy for walking and back issues. The return of physical symptoms coincided with starting a new relationship. The client finds meaning and identity in intellectual pursuits, particularly mathematics, and uses academic engagement as a way to cope with physical limitations. They have described feeling 'in complete darkness' before breakthroughs, drawing parallels between intellectual and physical struggles.
intermediate_belief: If I show my pain or limitations in social situations, others will judge me or see me as less capable. I must push through and hide my struggles to avoid being seen as weak. My value comes from being able to contribute and keep up with others, even when I'm suffering.
intermediate_belief_depression: If my pain keeps me from participating or connecting with others, it means I'm failing and will always be left out. There's no point in trying if my body keeps betraying me and I can't be like everyone else.
coping_strategies: The client immerses themselves in intellectual activities, such as mathematics and tutoring, to distract from physical discomfort and maintain a sense of purpose. They may avoid discussing their pain in social settings and push themselves to participate despite discomfort. Physical activity, when possible, is used to clear their mind and temporarily escape from pain.
situation: Attending a Halloween party while in persistent pain.
auto_thought: Everyone will notice that I'm struggling and think I'm weak. I can't keep up, and I'll just end up embarrassing myself.
behavior: The client tries to mask their discomfort and act as if nothing is wrong. They may withdraw from more active parts of the party or avoid drawing attention to themselves. They focus on monitoring how others perceive them and limit their engagement.
